channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: NASA to launch rescue mission June 30 to save Swift space telescope from burning up in Earth's atmosphere - Space
item.description:
1. NASA to launch rescue mission June 30 to save Swift space telescope from burning up in Earth's atmosphere  Space
  2. NASA Aims to Catch a Falling Space Telescope and Push It Back Up  The New York Times
  3. NASA aims to save a sinking space telescope with a rendezvous in orbit  NBC News
  4. Launch of Mission to Boost NASA’s Swift Scrubs Due to Weather  NASA Science (.gov)
  5. NASA to bump telescope back into space to avoid it falling to Earth  Australian Broadcasting Corporation

  4. Investigating ancient human DNA preservation on cave walls and in rock art  Nature
  5. In a first, scientists extract ancient human DNA from cave walls  National Geographic

item.title: Fossil kept in drawer for decades turns out to be first ever Antarctica dinosaur bone - CNN
item.description:
1. Fossil kept in drawer for decades turns out to be first ever Antarctica dinosaur bone  CNN

  5. A Fossil From Antarctica Sat in a Drawer for 40 Years. It Turned Out to Be the First Dinosaur Bone Ever Found on the Continent  Smithsonian Magazine

item.title: Two ‘Super-Puff’ Planets Lighter Than Candy Candy Discovered by Researchers - Good News Network

item.title: Comparative primate analysis shows that humans are not unique in having a tight cephalopelvic fit at birth - Nature
item.description:
1. Comparative primate analysis shows that humans are not unique in having a tight cephalopelvic fit at birth  Nature

  3. Childbirth for many primate species is even harder than for humans  New Scientist
  4. Childbirth is not uniquely difficult to humans  Phys.org
  5. Human Childbirth May Not Be Uniquely Difficult, as Some Squirrel Monkey Babies Have Heads Nearly Twice the Size of Their Mothers' Pelvic Space  Discover Magazine

item.title: In July 2025, the ATLAS sky survey in Chile spotted a faint comet racing through the solar system at roughly 130,000 miles per hour — and follow-up orbit calculations quickly showed it was not bound to the Sun at all, but had arrived from interstellar space. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In July 2025, the ATLAS sky survey in Chile spotted a faint comet racing through the solar system at roughly 130,000 miles per hour — and follow-up orbit calculations quickly showed it was not bound to the Sun at all, but had arrived from interstellar space.  Space Daily
  2. A Rare Comet Made History as the Third Known Interstellar Object to Fly Through Our Solar System. Studies Are Now Revealing the Mysterious Conditions in Which It Formed  Smithsonian Magazine
  3. California scientists scanned 3I/ATLAS for alien tech, and found only Earth-based noise  Yahoo
  4. 12 billion years old, this interstellar comet is older than our solar system  Phys.org
  5. Deuterium in Comets Tells Interesting Tales  Universe Today

item.title: Carl Sagan spent years trying to persuade NASA to take a picture many saw as scientifically pointless: Earth from more than 6 billion kilometres away, reduced to a pale blue dot smaller than a pixel. Voyager 1 finally captured it in 1990 — and that tiny speck be - Space Daily
item.description: Carl Sagan spent years trying to persuade NASA to take a picture many saw as scientifically pointless: Earth from more than 6 billion kilometres away, reduced to a pale blue dot smaller than a pixel. Voyager 1 finally captured it in 1990 — and that tiny speck be  Space Daily

  4. Ozone hole: Ozone layer damage began much earlier and by other substances  heise online
  5. Scientists say ozone loss could have been spotted in the 1950s, because today’s layered satellite tools re  The Economic Times

item.title: In January 2025, the loudest gravitational wave ever recorded hid a second signal nobody knew how to read — and a decade after the first detection, it has just given physicists the spin rate of a black hole's event horizon - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In January 2025, the loudest gravitational wave ever recorded hid a second signal nobody knew how to read — and a decade after the first detection, it has just given physicists the spin rate of a black hole's event horizon  Space Daily
  2. GW250114 reveals signatures of post-merger black-hole horizon  Nature
  3. Listening to the One Place That Swallows Everything  Universe Today
  4. Black hole's 'point of no escape' studied with the loudest gravitational waves ever heard  Space
  5. What happens at the edge of a black hole? Astronomers may be close to finding out  Scientific American

item.description:

item.title: The James Webb Space Telescope peered into one of the universe's oldest galaxy clusters, and scientists can't explain what they saw - Space
item.description:
1. The James Webb Space Telescope peered into one of the universe's oldest galaxy clusters, and scientists can't explain what they saw  Space
  2. The most distant lensing cluster reveals a dark matter surprise  Big Think
  3. The JWST Spies Six Galaxies Becoming One  Universe Today
  4. The James Webb Space Telescope found a spiral galaxy, nicknamed the Big Wheel, that existed just two billion years after the Big Bang — five times more massive than the Milky Way, with a spiral structure our models say couldn't have survived that early  Space Daily
  5. James Webb Space Telescope catches 6 galaxies merging into one of the largest galaxies in the universe  Yahoo
